draft.introduction: The Aeneid opens with Aeneas’s journey from fallen Troy to Italy, where he is destined to found the Roman race. Virgil combines the Odyssean wandering and Iliadic warfare into Rome’s founding epic.

translation.text: I sing of arms and the man who, fated to be an exile, was the first to come from the coasts of Troy to Italy and the Lavinian shores—much buffeted on land and sea by the power of the gods above, on account of the relentless anger of cruel Juno. He suffered much in war also, until he could found a city and bring his gods to Latium.
translation.original_text: Arma virumque cano, Troiae qui primus ab oris Italiam fato profugus Laviniaque venit litora

translator_notes.note: The opening words Arma virumque (arms and the man) echo both the Iliad (arms/war) and the Odyssey (the man/wandering), signaling that Virgil intends to surpass both Homeric poems. The word fato (by fate) introduces the Roman obsession with destiny that pervades the epic.
